title: Wing clipping chickens
subtitle: Reducing lift on one wing to keep free-range hens inside the garden.
excerpt: Clipping one wing takes two minutes and keeps most breeds contained in a garden where the fence height alone is not enough. This covers which feathers to cut, how far to cut them, and when the clip needs redoing after a moult.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code of Practice for Laying Hens (2018). Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive. BHWT Henkeeper guidance notes.

content.text: Wing clipping is the simplest physical barrier against a hen that has learned to clear a fence. Cutting the

on one wing only creates an imbalance: when the bird tries to fly, the unclipped wing generates more lift than the clipped side and the bird veers down. One wing rather than both is more effective for this reason. The clip is temporary: after the next annual
the feathers regrow and the process repeats.

Catch the bird in the coop at night when she is calm and settled on the perch. Hold her under one arm, with the wing to be clipped facing outward, the body pressed against your side. The wing should extend naturally. A bird held correctly does not flap. If the hen is struggling, tuck her head gently under your arm; darkness calms most birds quickly.

Extend the wing and locate the ten primary flight feathers. They run from the wrist joint outward along the leading edge. Beneath each feather is a blood-filled shaft (the blood quill) for the first few centimetres of growth. If the feather has a pink or dark base when held to the light, it is still growing: do not cut it. Cut only feathers with a fully white or clear shaft at the base, which indicates the blood supply has withdrawn.

Cut the primaries to roughly half their length, following the natural line of the shorter secondary feathers so the cut edge is not obvious from the side. A straight cut across all ten primaries is the standard approach. Take off no more than half the feather length at a time; the shorter the remaining shaft, the less lift the bird generates.

A blood quill that is cut will bleed. If this happens, apply pressure for 30 seconds with a clean cloth. Blood quills that continue bleeding after a few minutes need cutting closer to the base to stop bleeding; this is uncomfortable for the bird and avoidable by checking the shaft before cutting.

Wing clipping does not reliably contain determined or lightweight breeds: Leghorns and Araucanas in particular can clear a 1.5 m fence even with one wing clipped. For these breeds, a full covered run or a fence above 2 m is more reliable than clipping alone.
